(1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[:en:Hayashi Gahō|Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite book |last=Watanabe |first=Hiroshi |title=The architecture of Tokyo |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |date=April 25, 2001 |isbn=978-3-930698-93-6 }}
==External links== * [http://kaneiji.jp/ Kaneiji official website](Japanese) * [http://kaneiji.jp/about4 Kaneiji official website](English) * [https://archive.org/details/TokyoFieldRecordings/22_KaneijiTempleBell.mp3] 1959 recording of the Temple's bell at 6 AM from [[:en:The Internet Archive|The Internet Archive]]. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100124024254/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/arrange/attractions/facilities/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000eh6v.html Link to Japanese National Tourist Organization web page] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20061016125929/http://www.glasssteelandstone.com/BuildingDetail/4131.php Link to contemporary snapshot images of Kan'ei-ji five-story pagoda]}} * National Diet Library: [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/307/index.html photograph of Kan'ei-ji pagoda (1896)]; [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/306/index.html?type=Category&p=temples_shrines photograph of Toshogu Shrine at Ueno (1911)] * [http://www.nga.gov.au/MonetJapan/Detail.cfm?WorkID=J86 Link to "The battle of Toeizan Temple on Mount Sanno at Ueno," Yoshitoshi Tsukioka (1874)]

His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>
== See also == * [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]]

(1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[:en:Hayashi Gahō|Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite book |last=Watanabe |first=Hiroshi |title=The architecture of Tokyo |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |date=April 25, 2001 |isbn=978-3-930698-93-6 }}
==External links== * [http://kaneiji.jp/ Kaneiji official website](Japanese) * [http://kaneiji.jp/about4 Kaneiji official website](English) * [https://archive.org/details/TokyoFieldRecordings/22_KaneijiTempleBell.mp3] 1959 recording of the Temple's bell at 6 AM from [[:en:The Internet Archive|The Internet Archive]]. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100124024254/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/arrange/attractions/facilities/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000eh6v.html Link to Japanese National Tourist Organization web page] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20061016125929/http://www.glasssteelandstone.com/BuildingDetail/4131.php Link to contemporary snapshot images of Kan'ei-ji five-story pagoda]}} * National Diet Library: [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/307/index.html photograph of Kan'ei-ji pagoda (1896)]; [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/306/index.html?type=Category&p=temples_shrines photograph of Toshogu Shrine at Ueno (1911)] * [http://www.nga.gov.au/MonetJapan/Detail.cfm?WorkID=J86 Link to "The battle of Toeizan Temple on Mount Sanno at Ueno," Yoshitoshi Tsukioka (1874)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Five-storied Pagoda - Kan'ei-ji.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Ueno-Sakuragi|WD=Q11360373}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.721899|N|139.774705|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Toyoko, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=April 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in | native_name = | image = Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan'ei-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kan'ei-ji's original five-storied [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] in Ueno | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Uenosakuragi 1-14-11, Taito-ku, Tokyo | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kaneiji.jp }} {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] | year_completed = 1625 }} {{nihongo|'''Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in'''|東叡山寛永寺円頓院}} (also spelled Kan'eiji or Kaneiji) is a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], founded in 1625 during the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] era by [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], in an attempt to emulate the powerful religious center [[Enryaku-ji]], in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The main object of worship is {{nihongo|[[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|''Yakushirurikō Nyorai'']]|薬師瑠璃光如来}}.<ref name="taikei">Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei</ref>
It was named in a reference both to the Enryaku-ji's location atop [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] (''Tōeizan'' means "Mount Hiei of the East"), and also after the era during which it was erected, like Enryaku-ji (named after the Enryaku year period).<ref>Henry D. Smith, "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo", Braziller, 1986; plate 11 commentary</ref> Because it was one of the two [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] ''[[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]]'' (funeral temple; the other was [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]) and because it was destroyed in the closing days of the war that put an end to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it is inextricably linked to the Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s''.
Once a great complex, it used to occupy the entire heights north and east of [[:en:Shinobazu Pond|Shinobazu Pond]] and the plains where [[:en:Ueno Station|Ueno Station]] now stands.<ref name="seiden117">Seidensticker (1991:117)</ref> It had immense wealth, power and prestige, and it once consisted of over 30 buildings. Of the 15 Tokugawa [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s, six are buried here.<ref name="seiden117"/>
Many temple structures were destroyed in the [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|great Meireki fire]] of 1657.<ref name="col">[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/ealac/V3613/edo/html/kaneiji.html Kaneiji]. Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s [[:en:Battle of Ueno|Battle of Ueno]] and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by [[:en:Ueno Park|Ueno Park]].<ref name="seiden117"/>
What is today the temple's main hall was taken from [[:en:Kita-in|Kita-in]] in [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]] ([[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) and transferred to the site of a former Kan'ei-ji subtemple. Kan'ei-ji's five-story pagoda (photo above) and the [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]] shrine were amongst the gems of the old temple enclosure. Both stand undisturbed by the passage of years since the end of the Tokugawa shogunate.
The Shinobazu Pond itself and the Bentendō Temple which stands on its island used to be an integral part of Kan'ei-ji. Tenkai, liking [[:en:Lake Biwa|Lake Biwa]], had Benten Island built in imitation of Chikubushima, and then the Bentendō on it.<ref name="nagasaki">Japanese Old Photographs in Bakumatsu/Meiji Period, [http://oldphoto.lb.nagasaki-u.ac.jp/en/target.php?id=2098 Shinobazu Pond], accessed on November 16, 2008</ref> At the time the island was accessible only by boat, but later a stone bridge was added on the east, making it possible to walk to it.<ref name="nagasaki"/> The Bentendō Temple was destroyed during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], and the present one is a reconstruction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
== History == Tenkai wanted to create a powerful religious center and, to achieve that, he built Kan'ei-ji imitating [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="Watanabe"/> The temple was therefore erected north-east of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] to ward off evil spirits that were believed to come from that unlucky direction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
Tenkai's project enjoyed from the beginning the shogunate support, so much so that [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Tokugawa Hidetada]] in 1622 donated the land on which it was built.<ref name="taikei"/> At the time, on that land there were the suburban residences of three ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'', ([[:en:Tōdō Takatora|Tōdō Takatora]] of the [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu domain]], [[:en:Tsugaru Nobuhira|Tsugaru Nobuhira]] of the [[:en:Hirosaki Domain|Hirosaki domain]] and [[Hori Naoyori]] of the [[:en:Murakami Domain|Murakami domain]]), but the land was expropriated and donated to Tenkai for the temple.<ref name="taikei"/> He was also given 50 thousand silver [[:en:Ryō|Ryō]] and a building as a contribution.<ref name="taikei"/>
The chief abbot's residence, the ''Honbō'', was built in 1625, which is considered the year of foundation of the temple. After that, several ''daimyōs'' contributed with the construction of other buildings.<ref name="Watanabe"/> The main hall, called as in Enryaku-ji's case ''Konponchūdō'', was finished only in 1697.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
In 1643, after Tenkai's death, disciple Kōkai took his place. His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>

Kan'ei-ji.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Ueno-Sakuragi|WD=Q11360373}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.721899|N|139.774705|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Toyoko, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=April 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in | native_name = | image = Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan'ei-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kan'ei-ji's original five-storied [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] in Ueno | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Uenosakuragi 1-14-11, Taito-ku, Tokyo | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kaneiji.jp }} {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] | year_completed = 1625 }} {{nihongo|'''Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in'''|東叡山寛永寺円頓院}} (also spelled Kan'eiji or Kaneiji) is a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], founded in 1625 during the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] era by [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], in an attempt to emulate the powerful religious center [[Enryaku-ji]], in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The main object of worship is {{nihongo|[[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|''Yakushirurikō Nyorai'']]|薬師瑠璃光如来}}.<ref name="taikei">Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei</ref>
It was named in a reference both to the Enryaku-ji's location atop [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] (''Tōeizan'' means "Mount Hiei of the East"), and also after the era during which it was erected, like Enryaku-ji (named after the Enryaku year period).<ref>Henry D. Smith, "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo", Braziller, 1986; plate 11 commentary</ref> Because it was one of the two [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] ''[[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]]'' (funeral temple; the other was [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]) and because it was destroyed in the closing days of the war that put an end to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it is inextricably linked to the Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s''.
Once a great complex, it used to occupy the entire heights north and east of [[:en:Shinobazu Pond|Shinobazu Pond]] and the plains where [[:en:Ueno Station|Ueno Station]] now stands.<ref name="seiden117">Seidensticker (1991:117)</ref> It had immense wealth, power and prestige, and it once consisted of over 30 buildings. Of the 15 Tokugawa [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s, six are buried here.<ref name="seiden117"/>
Many temple structures were destroyed in the [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|great Meireki fire]] of 1657.<ref name="col">[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/ealac/V3613/edo/html/kaneiji.html Kaneiji]. Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s [[:en:Battle of Ueno|Battle of Ueno]] and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by [[:en:Ueno Park|Ueno Park]].<ref name="seiden117"/>
What is today the temple's main hall was taken from [[:en:Kita-in|Kita-in]] in [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]] ([[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) and transferred to the site of a former Kan'ei-ji subtemple. Kan'ei-ji's five-story pagoda (photo above) and the [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]] shrine were amongst the gems of the old temple enclosure. Both stand undisturbed by the passage of years since the end of the Tokugawa shogunate.
The Shinobazu Pond itself and the Bentendō Temple which stands on its island used to be an integral part of Kan'ei-ji. Tenkai, liking [[:en:Lake Biwa|Lake Biwa]], had Benten Island built in imitation of Chikubushima, and then the Bentendō on it.<ref name="nagasaki">Japanese Old Photographs in Bakumatsu/Meiji Period, [http://oldphoto.lb.nagasaki-u.ac.jp/en/target.php?id=2098 Shinobazu Pond], accessed on November 16, 2008</ref> At the time the island was accessible only by boat, but later a stone bridge was added on the east, making it possible to walk to it.<ref name="nagasaki"/> The Bentendō Temple was destroyed during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], and the present one is a reconstruction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
== History == Tenkai wanted to create a powerful religious center and, to achieve that, he built Kan'ei-ji imitating [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="Watanabe"/> The temple was therefore erected north-east of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] to ward off evil spirits that were believed to come from that unlucky direction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
Tenkai's project enjoyed from the beginning the shogunate support, so much so that [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Tokugawa Hidetada]] in 1622 donated the land on which it was built.<ref name="taikei"/> At the time, on that land there were the suburban residences of three ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'', ([[:en:Tōdō Takatora|Tōdō Takatora]] of the [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu domain]], [[:en:Tsugaru Nobuhira|Tsugaru Nobuhira]] of the [[:en:Hirosaki Domain|Hirosaki domain]] and [[Hori Naoyori]] of the [[:en:Murakami Domain|Murakami domain]]), but the land was expropriated and donated to Tenkai for the temple.<ref name="taikei"/> He was also given 50 thousand silver [[:en:Ryō|Ryō]] and a building as a contribution.<ref name="taikei"/>
The chief abbot's residence, the ''Honbō'', was built in 1625, which is considered the year of foundation of the temple. After that, several ''daimyōs'' contributed with the construction of other buildings.<ref name="Watanabe"/> The main hall, called as in Enryaku-ji's case ''Konponchūdō'', was finished only in 1697.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
In 1643, after Tenkai's death, disciple Kōkai took his place. His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>
== See also == * [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|2}}
==References== * {{cite web |url=http://www.japanknowledge.com |title=Kan'ei-ji |last={{nihongo|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|日本歴史地名大係}}, online version |publisher=Heibonsha |language=Japanese |access-date=November 17, 2008 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081107073644/http://www.japanknowledge.com/ |archive-date=November 7, 2008 |url-status=dead }} * {{cite book |last=Seidensticker |first=Edward |title=Low City, High City: Tokyo from Edo to the Earthquake: how the shogun's ancient capital became a great modern city, 1867–1923 |year=1991 |publisher=Harvard University Press |isbn=978-0-674-53939-6 }} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh]], Isaac. (1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[:en:Hayashi Gahō|Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite book |last=Watanabe |first=Hiroshi |title=The architecture of Tokyo |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |date=April 25, 2001 |isbn=978-3-930698-93-6 }}
==External links== * [http://kaneiji.jp/ Kaneiji official website](Japanese) * [http://kaneiji.jp/about4 Kaneiji official website](English) * [https://archive.org/details/TokyoFieldRecordings/22_KaneijiTempleBell.mp3] 1959 recording of the Temple's bell at 6 AM from [[:en:The Internet Archive|The Internet Archive]]. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100124024254/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/arrange/attractions/facilities/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000eh6v.html Link to Japanese National Tourist Organization web page] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20061016125929/http://www.glasssteelandstone.com/BuildingDetail/4131.php Link to contemporary snapshot images of Kan'ei-ji five-story pagoda]}} * National Diet Library: [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/307/index.html photograph of Kan'ei-ji pagoda (1896)]; [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/306/index.html?type=Category&p=temples_shrines photograph of Toshogu Shrine at Ueno (1911)] * [http://www.nga.gov.au/MonetJapan/Detail.cfm?WorkID=J86 Link to "The battle of Toeizan Temple on Mount Sanno at Ueno," Yoshitoshi Tsukioka (1874)]

Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s [[:en:Battle of Ueno|Battle of Ueno]] and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by [[:en:Ueno Park|Ueno Park]].<ref name="seiden117"/>

His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>
== See also == * [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]]

(1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[:en:Hayashi Gahō|Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite book |last=Watanabe |first=Hiroshi |title=The architecture of Tokyo |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |date=April 25, 2001 |isbn=978-3-930698-93-6 }}
==External links== * [http://kaneiji.jp/ Kaneiji official website](Japanese) * [http://kaneiji.jp/about4 Kaneiji official website](English) * [https://archive.org/details/TokyoFieldRecordings/22_KaneijiTempleBell.mp3] 1959 recording of the Temple's bell at 6 AM from [[:en:The Internet Archive|The Internet Archive]]. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100124024254/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/arrange/attractions/facilities/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000eh6v.html Link to Japanese National Tourist Organization web page] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20061016125929/http://www.glasssteelandstone.com/BuildingDetail/4131.php Link to contemporary snapshot images of Kan'ei-ji five-story pagoda]}} * National Diet Library: [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/307/index.html photograph of Kan'ei-ji pagoda (1896)]; [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/306/index.html?type=Category&p=temples_shrines photograph of Toshogu Shrine at Ueno (1911)] * [http://www.nga.gov.au/MonetJapan/Detail.cfm?WorkID=J86 Link to "The battle of Toeizan Temple on Mount Sanno at Ueno," Yoshitoshi Tsukioka (1874)]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kan'ei-ji]][[de:Kan’ei-ji]][[en:Kan'ei-ji]][[eu:Kan'ei-ji]][[fa:معبد کانئی]][[fi:Kan’ei-ji]][[fr:Kan'ei-ji]][[ja:寛永寺]][[ko:간에이지]][[zh:寬永寺]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Five-storied Pagoda - Kan'ei-ji.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Ueno-Sakuragi|WD=Q11360373}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.721899|N|139.774705|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Toyoko, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=April 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in | native_name = | image = Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan'ei-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kan'ei-ji's original five-storied [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] in Ueno | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Uenosakuragi 1-14-11, Taito-ku, Tokyo | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kaneiji.jp }} {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] | year_completed = 1625 }} {{nihongo|'''Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in'''|東叡山寛永寺円頓院}} (also spelled Kan'eiji or Kaneiji) is a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], founded in 1625 during the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] era by [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], in an attempt to emulate the powerful religious center [[Enryaku-ji]], in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The main object of worship is {{nihongo|[[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|''Yakushirurikō Nyorai'']]|薬師瑠璃光如来}}.<ref name="taikei">Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei</ref>
It was named in a reference both to the Enryaku-ji's location atop [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] (''Tōeizan'' means "Mount Hiei of the East"), and also after the era during which it was erected, like Enryaku-ji (named after the Enryaku year period).<ref>Henry D. Smith, "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo", Braziller, 1986; plate 11 commentary</ref> Because it was one of the two [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] ''[[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]]'' (funeral temple; the other was [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]) and because it was destroyed in the closing days of the war that put an end to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it is inextricably linked to the Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s''.
Once a great complex, it used to occupy the entire heights north and east of [[:en:Shinobazu Pond|Shinobazu Pond]] and the plains where [[:en:Ueno Station|Ueno Station]] now stands.<ref name="seiden117">Seidensticker (1991:117)</ref> It had immense wealth, power and prestige, and it once consisted of over 30 buildings. Of the 15 Tokugawa [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s, six are buried here.<ref name="seiden117"/>
Many temple structures were destroyed in the [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|great Meireki fire]] of 1657.<ref name="col">[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/ealac/V3613/edo/html/kaneiji.html Kaneiji]. Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s [[:en:Battle of Ueno|Battle of Ueno]] and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by [[:en:Ueno Park|Ueno Park]].<ref name="seiden117"/>
What is today the temple's main hall was taken from [[:en:Kita-in|Kita-in]] in [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]] ([[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) and transferred to the site of a former Kan'ei-ji subtemple. Kan'ei-ji's five-story pagoda (photo above) and the [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]] shrine were amongst the gems of the old temple enclosure. Both stand undisturbed by the passage of years since the end of the Tokugawa shogunate.
The Shinobazu Pond itself and the Bentendō Temple which stands on its island used to be an integral part of Kan'ei-ji. Tenkai, liking [[:en:Lake Biwa|Lake Biwa]], had Benten Island built in imitation of Chikubushima, and then the Bentendō on it.<ref name="nagasaki">Japanese Old Photographs in Bakumatsu/Meiji Period, [http://oldphoto.lb.nagasaki-u.ac.jp/en/target.php?id=2098 Shinobazu Pond], accessed on November 16, 2008</ref> At the time the island was accessible only by boat, but later a stone bridge was added on the east, making it possible to walk to it.<ref name="nagasaki"/> The Bentendō Temple was destroyed during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], and the present one is a reconstruction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
== History == Tenkai wanted to create a powerful religious center and, to achieve that, he built Kan'ei-ji imitating [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="Watanabe"/> The temple was therefore erected north-east of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] to ward off evil spirits that were believed to come from that unlucky direction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
Tenkai's project enjoyed from the beginning the shogunate support, so much so that [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Tokugawa Hidetada]] in 1622 donated the land on which it was built.<ref name="taikei"/> At the time, on that land there were the suburban residences of three ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'', ([[:en:Tōdō Takatora|Tōdō Takatora]] of the [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu domain]], [[:en:Tsugaru Nobuhira|Tsugaru Nobuhira]] of the [[:en:Hirosaki Domain|Hirosaki domain]] and [[Hori Naoyori]] of the [[:en:Murakami Domain|Murakami domain]]), but the land was expropriated and donated to Tenkai for the temple.<ref name="taikei"/> He was also given 50 thousand silver [[:en:Ryō|Ryō]] and a building as a contribution.<ref name="taikei"/>
The chief abbot's residence, the ''Honbō'', was built in 1625, which is considered the year of foundation of the temple. After that, several ''daimyōs'' contributed with the construction of other buildings.<ref name="Watanabe"/> The main hall, called as in Enryaku-ji's case ''Konponchūdō'', was finished only in 1697.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
In 1643, after Tenkai's death, disciple Kōkai took his place. His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>

Kan'ei-ji.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Ueno-Sakuragi|WD=Q11360373}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.721899|N|139.774705|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Toyoko, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=April 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in | native_name = | image = Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan'ei-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kan'ei-ji's original five-storied [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] in Ueno | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Uenosakuragi 1-14-11, Taito-ku, Tokyo | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] | deity = | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kaneiji.jp }} {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Tokugawa Iemitsu]] | year_completed = 1625 }} {{nihongo|'''Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in'''|東叡山寛永寺円頓院}} (also spelled Kan'eiji or Kaneiji) is a [[:en:Tendai|Tendai]] [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhist]] temple in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]], founded in 1625 during the [[:en:Kan'ei|Kan'ei]] era by [[:en:Tenkai|Tenkai]], in an attempt to emulate the powerful religious center [[Enryaku-ji]], in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The main object of worship is {{nihongo|[[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|''Yakushirurikō Nyorai'']]|薬師瑠璃光如来}}.<ref name="taikei">Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei</ref>
It was named in a reference both to the Enryaku-ji's location atop [[:en:Mount Hiei|Mount Hiei]] (''Tōeizan'' means "Mount Hiei of the East"), and also after the era during which it was erected, like Enryaku-ji (named after the Enryaku year period).<ref>Henry D. Smith, "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo", Braziller, 1986; plate 11 commentary</ref> Because it was one of the two [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] ''[[:en:Bodaiji|Bodaiji]]'' (funeral temple; the other was [[:en:Zōjō-ji|Zōjō-ji]]) and because it was destroyed in the closing days of the war that put an end to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it is inextricably linked to the Tokugawa ''[[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s''.
Once a great complex, it used to occupy the entire heights north and east of [[:en:Shinobazu Pond|Shinobazu Pond]] and the plains where [[:en:Ueno Station|Ueno Station]] now stands.<ref name="seiden117">Seidensticker (1991:117)</ref> It had immense wealth, power and prestige, and it once consisted of over 30 buildings. Of the 15 Tokugawa [[:en:Shogun|Shogun]]s, six are buried here.<ref name="seiden117"/>
Many temple structures were destroyed in the [[:en:Great Fire of Meireki|great Meireki fire]] of 1657.<ref name="col">[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/ealac/V3613/edo/html/kaneiji.html Kaneiji]. Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s [[:en:Battle of Ueno|Battle of Ueno]] and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by [[:en:Ueno Park|Ueno Park]].<ref name="seiden117"/>
What is today the temple's main hall was taken from [[:en:Kita-in|Kita-in]] in [[:en:Kawagoe, Saitama|Kawagoe]] ([[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]]) and transferred to the site of a former Kan'ei-ji subtemple. Kan'ei-ji's five-story pagoda (photo above) and the [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]] shrine were amongst the gems of the old temple enclosure. Both stand undisturbed by the passage of years since the end of the Tokugawa shogunate.
The Shinobazu Pond itself and the Bentendō Temple which stands on its island used to be an integral part of Kan'ei-ji. Tenkai, liking [[:en:Lake Biwa|Lake Biwa]], had Benten Island built in imitation of Chikubushima, and then the Bentendō on it.<ref name="nagasaki">Japanese Old Photographs in Bakumatsu/Meiji Period, [http://oldphoto.lb.nagasaki-u.ac.jp/en/target.php?id=2098 Shinobazu Pond], accessed on November 16, 2008</ref> At the time the island was accessible only by boat, but later a stone bridge was added on the east, making it possible to walk to it.<ref name="nagasaki"/> The Bentendō Temple was destroyed during [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], and the present one is a reconstruction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
== History == Tenkai wanted to create a powerful religious center and, to achieve that, he built Kan'ei-ji imitating [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="Watanabe"/> The temple was therefore erected north-east of [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] to ward off evil spirits that were believed to come from that unlucky direction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
Tenkai's project enjoyed from the beginning the shogunate support, so much so that [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Tokugawa Hidetada]] in 1622 donated the land on which it was built.<ref name="taikei"/> At the time, on that land there were the suburban residences of three ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'', ([[:en:Tōdō Takatora|Tōdō Takatora]] of the [[:en:Tsu Domain|Tsu domain]], [[:en:Tsugaru Nobuhira|Tsugaru Nobuhira]] of the [[:en:Hirosaki Domain|Hirosaki domain]] and [[Hori Naoyori]] of the [[:en:Murakami Domain|Murakami domain]]), but the land was expropriated and donated to Tenkai for the temple.<ref name="taikei"/> He was also given 50 thousand silver [[:en:Ryō|Ryō]] and a building as a contribution.<ref name="taikei"/>
The chief abbot's residence, the ''Honbō'', was built in 1625, which is considered the year of foundation of the temple. After that, several ''daimyōs'' contributed with the construction of other buildings.<ref name="Watanabe"/> The main hall, called as in Enryaku-ji's case ''Konponchūdō'', was finished only in 1697.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
In 1643, after Tenkai's death, disciple Kōkai took his place. His successor was [[:en:Emperor Go-Mizunoo|Emperor Go-Mizunoo]]'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun [[:en:Tokugawa Hidetada|Hidetada]] rests. His successor [[:en:Tokugawa Iemitsu|Iemitsu]] sent his remains to Nikkō because the [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]], mausoleum of [[:en:Tokugawa Ieyasu|Tokugawa Ieyasu]], founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]] and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in [[:en:Nikkō|Nikkō]]) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby [[:en:Yanaka Cemetery|Yanaka Cemetery]]), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]] are interred [[:en:Tokugawa Ietsuna|Tokugawa Ietsuna]], [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ieharu|Tokugawa Ieharu]], [[:en:Tokugawa Ienari|Tokugawa Ienari]], [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]] and Iesada's wife [[:en:Tenshō-in|Tenshō-in]].<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband [[:en:Tokugawa Iesada|Tokugawa Iesada]].
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist [[:en:Edward Seidensticker|Edward Seidensticker]] describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the [[:en:Shōgitai|Shōgitai]], a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the [[:en:Satsuma Domain|Satsuma]] and [[:en:Chōshū Domain|Chōshū]] revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>
== See also == * [[:en:Ueno Tōshō-gū|Ueno Tōshō-gū]]
==Notes== {{Reflist|2}}

(1834). [Siyun-sai Rin-siyo/[[:en:Hayashi Gahō|Hayashi Gahō]], 1652], ''[[:en:Nipon o daï itsi ran|Nipon o daï itsi ran]]; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran Annales des empereurs du Japon.]'' Paris: [[:en:Royal Asiatic Society|Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland]]. * {{cite book |last=Watanabe |first=Hiroshi |title=The architecture of Tokyo |publisher=Edition Axel Menges |date=April 25, 2001 |isbn=978-3-930698-93-6 }}
==External links== * [http://kaneiji.jp/ Kaneiji official website](Japanese) * [http://kaneiji.jp/about4 Kaneiji official website](English) * [https://archive.org/details/TokyoFieldRecordings/22_KaneijiTempleBell.mp3] 1959 recording of the Temple's bell at 6 AM from [[:en:The Internet Archive|The Internet Archive]]. * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100124024254/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/arrange/attractions/facilities/shrines_temples/83dn3a000000eh6v.html Link to Japanese National Tourist Organization web page] * {{usurped|1=[https://web.archive.org/web/20061016125929/http://www.glasssteelandstone.com/BuildingDetail/4131.php Link to contemporary snapshot images of Kan'ei-ji five-story pagoda]}} * National Diet Library: [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/307/index.html photograph of Kan'ei-ji pagoda (1896)]; [http://www.ndl.go.jp/scenery/e/data/306/index.html?type=Category&p=temples_shrines photograph of Toshogu Shrine at Ueno (1911)] * [http://www.nga.gov.au/MonetJapan/Detail.cfm?WorkID=J86 Link to "The battle of Toeizan Temple on Mount Sanno at Ueno," Yoshitoshi Tsukioka (1874)]

Kan'ei-ji.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Tendai|WD=Q855964}}|prefecture={{ill|Ueno-Sakuragi|WD=Q11360373}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.721899|N|139.774705|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310}}|deity={{ill|Bhaiṣajyaguru Buddha|WD=Q854773}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3120766]] Wikidata name: Kan'ei-ji [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Toyoko, Japan}} {{Use mdy dates|date=April 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in | native_name = | image = Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan'ei-ji.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kan'ei-ji's original five-storied [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] in Ueno | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = Uenosakuragi 1-14-11, Taito-ku, Tokyo | coordinates = | religious_affiliation = {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} | deity = | country = Japan | functional_status = | website = {{URL|http://www.kaneiji.jp }} {{in lang|ja}} | founded_by = {{ill|Tenkai|en|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310|lt=Tenkai}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Tokugawa Iemitsu}} | year_completed = 1625 }} {{nihongo|'''Tōeizan Kan'ei-ji Endon-in'''|東叡山寛永寺円頓院}} (also spelled Kan'eiji or Kaneiji) is a {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|WD=Q855964|lt=Tendai}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} temple in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, founded in 1625 during the {{ill|Kan'ei|en|Kan'ei|WD=Q1192913|lt=Kan'ei}} era by {{ill|Tenkai|en|Tenkai|WD=Q1142310|lt=Tenkai}}, in an attempt to emulate the powerful religious center [[Enryaku-ji]], in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The main object of worship is {{nihongo|[[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|''Yakushirurikō Nyorai'']]|薬師瑠璃光如来}}.<ref name="taikei">Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei</ref>
It was named in a reference both to the Enryaku-ji's location atop {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|WD=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei}} (''Tōeizan'' means "Mount Hiei of the East"), and also after the era during which it was erected, like Enryaku-ji (named after the Enryaku year period).<ref>Henry D. Smith, "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo", Braziller, 1986; plate 11 commentary</ref> Because it was one of the two [[Tokugawa clan|Tokugawa]] ''{{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|WD=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji}}'' (funeral temple; the other was {{ill|Zōjō-ji|en|Zōjō-ji|WD=Q249139|lt=Zōjō-ji}}) and because it was destroyed in the closing days of the war that put an end to the [[Tokugawa shogunate]], it is inextricably linked to the Tokugawa ''{{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s''.
Once a great complex, it used to occupy the entire heights north and east of {{ill|Shinobazu Pond|en|Shinobazu Pond|WD=Q5365093|lt=Shinobazu Pond}} and the plains where {{ill|Ueno Station|en|Ueno Station|WD=Q801551|lt=Ueno Station}} now stands.<ref name="seiden117">Seidensticker (1991:117)</ref> It had immense wealth, power and prestige, and it once consisted of over 30 buildings. Of the 15 Tokugawa {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun}}s, six are buried here.<ref name="seiden117"/>
Many temple structures were destroyed in the {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|WD=Q1336847|lt=great Meireki fire}} of 1657.<ref name="col">[http://www.columbia.edu/itc/ealac/V3613/edo/html/kaneiji.html Kaneiji]. Columbia University. Accessed June 13, 2008.</ref> A new hall was constructed inside the enclosure of Kan'ei-ji in 1698.<ref>Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://archive.org/details/niponodaitsiran01ringoog ''Annales des empereurs du Japon,'' p. 415.]</ref> The temple and its numerous annexes were almost completely destroyed during the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}'s {{ill|Battle of Ueno|en|Battle of Ueno|WD=Q715562|lt=Battle of Ueno}} and never restored.<ref name="seiden"/> Much of the site where it once stood was confiscated and is now occupied by {{ill|Ueno Park|en|Ueno Park|WD=Q746216|lt=Ueno Park}}.<ref name="seiden117"/>
What is today the temple's main hall was taken from {{ill|Kita-in|en|Kita-in|WD=Q3080561|lt=Kita-in}} in {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|WD=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe}} ({{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}) and transferred to the site of a former Kan'ei-ji subtemple. Kan'ei-ji's five-story pagoda (photo above) and the {{ill|Ueno Tōshō-gū|en|Ueno Tōshō-gū|WD=Q708550|lt=Ueno Tōshō-gū}} shrine were amongst the gems of the old temple enclosure. Both stand undisturbed by the passage of years since the end of the Tokugawa shogunate.
The Shinobazu Pond itself and the Bentendō Temple which stands on its island used to be an integral part of Kan'ei-ji. Tenkai, liking {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|WD=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa}}, had Benten Island built in imitation of Chikubushima, and then the Bentendō on it.<ref name="nagasaki">Japanese Old Photographs in Bakumatsu/Meiji Period, [http://oldphoto.lb.nagasaki-u.ac.jp/en/target.php?id=2098 Shinobazu Pond], accessed on November 16, 2008</ref> At the time the island was accessible only by boat, but later a stone bridge was added on the east, making it possible to walk to it.<ref name="nagasaki"/> The Bentendō Temple was destroyed during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, and the present one is a reconstruction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
== History == Tenkai wanted to create a powerful religious center and, to achieve that, he built Kan'ei-ji imitating [[Enryaku-ji]].<ref name="Watanabe"/> The temple was therefore erected north-east of {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} to ward off evil spirits that were believed to come from that unlucky direction.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
Tenkai's project enjoyed from the beginning the shogunate support, so much so that {{ill|Tokugawa Hidetada|en|Tokugawa Hidetada|WD=Q314464|lt=Tokugawa Hidetada}} in 1622 donated the land on which it was built.<ref name="taikei"/> At the time, on that land there were the suburban residences of three ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]s'', ({{ill|Tōdō Takatora|en|Tōdō Takatora|WD=Q1377984|lt=Tōdō Takatora}} of the {{ill|Tsu Domain|en|Tsu Domain|WD=Q1060551|lt=Tsu domain}}, {{ill|Tsugaru Nobuhira|en|Tsugaru Nobuhira|WD=Q837782|lt=Tsugaru Nobuhira}} of the {{ill|Hirosaki Domain|en|Hirosaki Domain|WD=Q903555|lt=Hirosaki domain}} and [[Hori Naoyori]] of the {{ill|Murakami Domain|en|Murakami Domain|WD=Q849483|lt=Murakami domain}}), but the land was expropriated and donated to Tenkai for the temple.<ref name="taikei"/> He was also given 50 thousand silver {{ill|Ryō|en|Ryō|WD=Q3057942|lt=Ryō}} and a building as a contribution.<ref name="taikei"/>
The chief abbot's residence, the ''Honbō'', was built in 1625, which is considered the year of foundation of the temple. After that, several ''daimyōs'' contributed with the construction of other buildings.<ref name="Watanabe"/> The main hall, called as in Enryaku-ji's case ''Konponchūdō'', was finished only in 1697.<ref name="Watanabe"/>
In 1643, after Tenkai's death, disciple Kōkai took his place. His successor was {{ill|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|en|Emperor Go-Mizunoo|WD=Q320229|lt=Emperor Go-Mizunoo}}'s third son Shuchōho Shinnō.<ref name="taikei"/> From then on until the end of the shogunate, Kan'ei-ji's chief abbots were chosen among the Emperor's children or favorite nephews and called with the honorific {{nihongo|''Rinnōjinomiya''|輪王寺宮}}.<ref name="taikei"/>
===Tokugawa and Kan'ei-ji=== With the favor of the Tokugawa the temple prospered but, at least in the first years since foundation, it was just the Tokugawa family temple, while the sole funeral temple of the Tokugawa was still Zōjō-ji, where the second shogun {{ill|Tokugawa Hidetada|en|Tokugawa Hidetada|WD=Q314464|lt=Hidetada}} rests. His successor {{ill|Tokugawa Iemitsu|en|Tokugawa Iemitsu|WD=Q314481|lt=Iemitsu}} sent his remains to Nikkō because the {{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}, mausoleum of {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}, founder of the dynasty, was there; he however also built a mausoleum at Kan'ei'ji.<ref name="taikei"/> After that, the fourth ''shōgun'' {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} and the fifth Tokugawa Tsunayoshi were put to rest in Ueno, and Kan'ei-ji became a Tokugawa funeral temple like Zōjō-ji.<ref name="taikei"/> Zōjō-ji didn't like the change, but after the next shogun Tokugawa Ienobu's mausoleum was built on its land, the custom became to alternate the temples at each generation, and that lasted until the closing of the shogunate era. Excepted Ieyasu and Iemitsu (buried in {{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}}) and last shogun [[Tokugawa Yoshinobu|Yoshinobu]] (also known as Keiki, buried in nearby {{ill|Yanaka Cemetery|en|Yanaka Cemetery|WD=Q864387|lt=Yanaka Cemetery}}), all of the Tokugawa ''shōguns'' are buried either at Zōjō-ji or Kan'ei-ji, six at one and six at the other. In what used to be the Kan'ei-ji cemetery near the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}} are interred {{ill|Tokugawa Ietsuna|en|Tokugawa Ietsuna|WD=Q323422|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna}}, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]], {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieharu|en|Tokugawa Ieharu|WD=Q437518|lt=Tokugawa Ieharu}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Ienari|en|Tokugawa Ienari|WD=Q439717|lt=Tokugawa Ienari}}, {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}} and Iesada's wife {{ill|Tenshō-in|en|Tenshō-in|WD=Q1063767|lt=Tenshō-in}}.<ref name="Watanabe">Watanabe (2005:30)</ref> Ietsuna's and Tsunayoshi's mausoleums were destroyed in 1945. The cemetery is closed to the public, but can be seen from the street.
The last visit of a Tokugawa shogunate member was on the 8 August 1863 by Tenshō-in, for the memorial service of her husband {{ill|Tokugawa Iesada|en|Tokugawa Iesada|WD=Q336383|lt=Tokugawa Iesada}}.
===Battle of Ueno and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji=== {{Main|Battle of Ueno}} [[File:Ueno Battlefield.JPG|thumb|upright=1.5|left|Devastation of Ueno after the battle. 1868 photograph.]]In his book ''High City, Low City'' Japanologist {{ill|Edward Seidensticker|en|Edward Seidensticker|WD=Q3272933|lt=Edward Seidensticker}} describes the last days and the destruction of Kan'ei-ji.
The revolutionary forces had occupied most of Tokyo, and Edo Castle and the majority of the Tokugawa troops had already surrendered, however one band of shogunate soldiers barricaded itself in Ueno with the intention to resist.<ref name="seiden">Seidensticker (1991:27)</ref> About 2000 men strong, it was composed of members of the {{ill|Shōgitai|en|Shōgitai|WD=Q710107|lt=Shōgitai}}, a military unit of former Tokugawa retainers.<ref name="Watanabe"/> They held the Kan'ei-ji's abbot in hostage, and maybe for this reason the {{ill|Satsuma Domain|en|Satsuma Domain|WD=Q676187|lt=Satsuma}} and {{ill|Chōshū Domain|en|Chōshū Domain|WD=Q876267|lt=Chōshū}} revolutionaries didn't attack immediately.<ref name="seiden"/>
On July 4, 1868 (''Meiji 1, 15th day of the 5th month''),<ref>[https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm NengoCalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=September 30, 2007 }}: 明治一年五月十五日; n.b., the old lunar calendar date, used for example by Watanabe, was misidentified as May 15.</ref> the final attack came and from early morning artillery rounds fell from Hongo's heights on Ueno.<ref name="seiden"/> After a fierce battle, in the late afternoon the revolutionary forces broke through the defenses in the south at the Black Gate (the ''Kuromon''), near what is today Ueno Park's entrance.<ref name="seiden"/> There were altogether about 300 dead, mostly defenders.<ref name="seiden"/> Most of the artillery rounds had gone astray, causing fires in which the whole Kan'ei-ji and up to a thousand houses were destroyed.<ref name="seiden"/> The temple's abbot fled in disguise and left the city by boat.<ref name="seiden"/>
